1: Elohi ha'Ivrim: "the God of the Hebrews." The full covenant title — "the LORD, the God of the Hebrews" — appears in each plague-introduction (Exodus 7:16; Exodus 8:1,20; Exodus 9:1,13; Exodus 10:3). YHWH is not merely a deity but the God specifically of this people, whose identity is tied to their release. The covenant name establishes that the plague is not merely a power demonstration but a covenant action on behalf of a specific people.
2: veodkha mechazik bam: "and continue to hold them." Chazaq (H2388, Hiphil) with Pharaoh as subject: "if you continue making yourself strong/firm in holding them." The hardening vocabulary (chazaq) and the grip vocabulary (chazaq) are the same word: Pharaoh's hard heart is his hard grip on Israel. The theological connection is explicit in the Hebrew.
3: yad YHWH hovah: "the hand of the LORD will be." Yad YHWH — the hand of the LORD — is the central metaphor of Exodus: the mighty hand that delivers (Exodus 13:9; Exodus 14:31; Exodus 15:6). Here applied as plague-instrument: the same divine hand that will deliver Israel through the sea first strikes Egypt's livestock. Dever (H1698, pestilence) — Habakkuk 3:5 personifies it marching before the divine warrior.
4: veheflah YHWH bein miqneh Yisrael: "the LORD will distinguish (pala) between Israel's livestock and Egypt's." Pala (H6395) for the second time (cf. Exodus 8:22). The remarkable precision of YHWH's protection — biological and geographic specificity — is the strongest single evidence-claim the plagues make: if every Egyptian field animal dies while not one Israelite animal dies, the distinction is supernatural.
5: vayasem YHWH moed: "the LORD set a time (moed)." Moed (H4150) — appointed time, set meeting; the vocabulary of sacred assembly (Lev 23). The use of moed for the plague's appointed moment carries liturgical weight: even the judgment is ordered by divine appointment, as sacred in its timing as Israel's own festival calendar.
6: lo met mimikneh venei Yisrael echad: "not one of the Israelites' animals had died." Total fulfillment of the distinction: every Egyptian field animal dead, zero Israelite animals dead. The precision creates an empirically testable claim (Pharaoh sends inspectors, Exodus 9:7). YHWH's word is verifiable by the evidence it generates.
7: vayikhbad lev Paroh: "Pharaoh's heart was heavy (kaved)." The inspectors' report confirms the miracle; Pharaoh receives verifiable proof and still hardens. The inverse relationship between evidence and responsiveness in Pharaoh — the more complete the proof, the heavier the heart — is the paradox the NT addresses in its hardening theology (John 12:40 quoting Isaiah 6:9-10; Romans 9:17-18).
8: piach kivshaim: "furnace soot (piach, H6368)." The kivshan (furnace, H3536) — the kiln used for brickmaking, the site of Israel's slave labor (cf. Genesis 19:28 for kivshan; implied throughout Exodus 1:14; 5). The sixth plague uses the physical material of Israelite oppression as the raw material of Egypt's judgment. The instrument of the slaves' suffering becomes the instrument of the slaveholders' affliction.
9: shechein poreiach avubot: "festering boils (avubot)." Shechein (H7822) — inflammation, boil; used of Job's affliction (Job 2:7), the Egyptian boils of Deuteronomy 28:27 as a covenant curse, and the plague of Revelation 16:2. The sixth plague enters the human body itself — unlike the previous environmental and animal plagues. The escalation from external to bodily affliction marks the second triad's culmination.
10: vayiz'reihu Mosheh hashamayimah: "Moses threw it into the air (toward the sky)." The heavenward gesture — throwing soot upward, toward the divine domain — and the dust falling on all Egypt below creates a visual image of divine judgment descending from heaven. Plague descends from above; salvation (the pillar of cloud/fire) also descends from above. The vertical axis is the theological axis of Exodus.
11: velo yakhlu hachartumaim la'amod lifnei Mosheh: "The magicians could not stand before Moses because of the boils." Their incapacitation by the very plague they helped multiply is ironic: they brought up more frogs (Exodus 8:7) and more gnats (Exodus 8:18, tried unsuccessfully) — now they cannot even present themselves in the contest. "Unable to stand" (amad) is the language of defeat in judicial or military contest. The Egyptian occult system is permanently incapacitated.
12: vayechazek YHWH et-lev Paroh: "But the LORD hardened (chazaq) Pharaoh's heart." The theological turning point: from 9:12 onward, YHWH is explicitly the subject of the hardening (Exodus 9:12; Exodus 10:1,20,27; Exodus 11:10; Exodus 14:4,8). The shift in agency does not mean God begins overriding Pharaoh's free will arbitrarily; it means Pharaoh's pattern of self-hardening through repeated choices has reached the threshold where God ratifies and seals what Pharaoh has already determined to be. Romans 1:24,26,28 uses the same pattern: "God gave them over" — divine confirmation of human choice.
13: hashkem baboker vehityatsev lifnei Paroh: "Get up early in the morning and stand before Pharaoh." The seventh plague (hail) begins the third triad. "This is what the LORD, the God of the Hebrews, says" — the covenant title occurs five times in the plague introductions. The hail is the most severe plague so far announced in advance; the announcement itself is part of the argument: YHWH announces precisely what he will do.
14: ki bapa'am hazot ani sholeiach et-kol-magefotai: "For this time I will send all my plagues against you personally." El-libkha — against your heart, i.e., against your very person. The escalation is explicit: previous plagues targeted the environment, animals, and servants; this one is directed at Pharaoh himself. "There is no one like me in all the earth" — the incomparability formula now claims universal scope, not merely supremacy over Egyptian deities.
15: ki atah shalakhti et-yadi vakek otekha: "For by now I could have stretched out my hand and struck you." The past potential — what God could have done already — establishes the restraint that has characterized the plague sequence. The plagues have been graduated, not maximal. YHWH's restraint is purposeful (Exodus 9:16), not a limitation of power.
16: he'emadtikha (heʿĕmadtîḵā): "I have raised you up/maintained you." Hiphil of amad (H5975, to stand) — to cause to stand, to establish, to maintain. The LXX translates anestesa se ("I raised you up"), which Paul quotes verbatim in Romans 9:17: "For the Scripture says to Pharaoh: 'For this very purpose I have raised you up, that I might show my power in you, and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth.'" The hardening of Pharaoh is embedded within YHWH's purpose for the universal proclamation of his name — the exodus serves a missionary goal.
17: mistateil beami: "setting yourself against my people." Salal (H5549) — to cast up, pile up (a road or mound), here to erect oneself as an obstacle. The image is of Pharaoh constructing himself as a physical barrier against Israel's departure. The verb's construction-imagery suggests not passive resistance but active, engineered opposition.
18: kaved meod asher lo-hayah kamohuu mitsrayim: "the most severe... that has ever fallen on Egypt from the day it was founded." The superlative framing establishes the hail as a historical marker. Egypt's antiquity is acknowledged; this storm exceeds anything in its long history. The claim of unprecedented severity is the hail's claim on Pharaoh's attention.
19: shelach hoykl et-miqnekha: "Give the order to bring your livestock to shelter." The advance warning before the hail — absent in the third plague of each previous triad (gnats, Exodus 8:16; boils, Exodus 9:8) — is unusual mercy: YHWH gives Pharaoh's own officials the option of protecting their possessions. The warning creates a division within Egypt between those who respond to YHWH's word (Exodus 9:20) and those who do not (Exodus 9:21).
20: hayare et-dvar YHWH: "Those who feared (yare) the word of the LORD." Yirah (H3372) — the fear/reverence of YHWH — appears within Pharaoh's own court, among non-Israelite Egyptians. The plague creates a theological division within Egypt itself. This anticipates the "mixed multitude" (erev rav, Exodus 12:38) that departs with Israel — Egyptians who align with YHWH's purpose.
21: va'asher lo sam libbo el-dvar YHWH: "those who did not set their heart on the word of the LORD." The heart-orientation (sam libbo) toward YHWH's word is the distinguishing marker between those who are protected and those who suffer. The lev (heart) that is "set on" the divine word is the response YHWH requires — the same heart-orientation that Pharaoh consistently refuses is freely given by unnamed Egyptian officials.
22: neteh et-yadkha al-hashamayim: "Stretch out your hand toward the sky." The upward gesture — toward the heavens — marks the hail as descending from the divine domain above. The combination of thunder (qolot, "voices/thunders"), hail, and fire (lightning) recalls the Sinai theophany (Exodus 19:16) and anticipates the cosmic disruptions of Revelation's trumpet and bowl judgments (Revelation 8:7; Exodus 16:21).
23: qolot (H6963): "thunder/voices." The same word for the thunder at Sinai (Exodus 19:16; Exodus 20:18) where YHWH speaks in qolot. The hailstorm's thunder is YHWH's voice, just as Sinai's thunder is YHWH's voice. Pharaoh will later acknowledge "voices of God" (qolot Elohim, Exodus 9:28) — using the divine plural for superlative intensity. The storm is not merely meteorological; it is a theophany.
24: uvar uaish mitlaqachat betokh habarad: "lightning flashing through it." Mitlaqachat — flashing, self-intermingling — describes fire and hail as supernaturally interwoven. Psalms 105:32-33 cites the hail plague: "He gave them hail for rain, and lightning throughout their land; he struck down their vines and their fig trees, and shattered the trees of their country." The Psalm's poetic rendering confirms the plague's status as paradigmatic divine judgment.
25: vayakh habarad bkhol-erets Mitsrayim: "The hail struck down everything in the fields throughout Egypt." The comprehensive destruction of the field environment — human laborers, animals, crops — is the culmination of the second triad's agricultural focus. The agricultural devastation of Egypt's labor-system (the one that enslaved Israel) is now being dismantled by the same God who commissioned it for his own purpose.
26: raq beerets Goshen: "Only in the land of Goshen." The third explicit Goshen exception (Exodus 8:22; Exodus 9:4,26). The geographic precision of YHWH's protection — a named region, not merely individual exemptions — is the plague narrative's most powerful evidence-claim. The visible boundary between Goshen and Egypt is a spatial argument for YHWH's sovereign control of the natural order.
27: chata'ti hapaam YHWH hatsadiq: "This time I have sinned. The LORD is righteous (tsadiq)." Pharaoh's confession applies the judicial vocabulary: tsadiq (righteous, in the right) to YHWH and resha'im (wicked, in the wrong) to himself and his people. The judicial framework — who is in the right — is the framework in which Moses has been operating throughout. Pharaoh finally articulates it correctly. Moses's response (Exodus 9:30: "I know you do not yet truly fear the LORD God") demonstrates that correct theological statement without genuine fear is insufficient.
28: qolot Elohim uvarad: "this deadly thunder (qolot Elohim)." Elohim used as a superlative intensifier (divine thunder = overwhelming/mighty thunder). The same construction appears in "mountains of God" (Psalms 36:6), "cedars of God" (Psalms 80:10). Pharaoh's language acknowledges the thunder as divinely overwhelming — he wants the divine voice to stop.
29: phros kapai el-YHWH: "I will spread out my hands to the LORD." The gesture of spreading palms upward (peres kaf) is the OT intercessory posture (1 Kings 8:22,38,54; Isaiah 1:15; Psalms 44:20). "The earth is the LORD's (laYHWH haarets)" — the creation-ownership formula grounds the prayer in YHWH's cosmic sovereignty. The one who owns the earth can stop its weather; the prayer is based on demonstrated ownership.
30: terem tiru mipnei YHWH Elohim: "you still do not truly fear the LORD God." Moses intercedes while knowing the confession is inadequate. Yirah (H3372) — the fear/reverence of YHWH — is the required disposition. Pharaoh acknowledges YHWH's righteousness (Exodus 9:27) and power (Exodus 9:28) without possessing genuine fear. The theological diagnosis: right information about YHWH without the right orientation toward him.
31: vehapishta vehase'orah nukah: "The flax and barley were ruined." The agronomic precision — flax and barley in bloom or heading, wheat and spelt not yet ripe — provides chronological specificity: the hail falls approximately in February (Egyptian calendar, month of Phamenoth). The detail authenticates the account as based on knowledge of Egyptian agricultural seasons and has been confirmed by Egyptologists' reconstructions of the Nile calendar.
32: afilot henah: "they ripen later." Afilot — late-ripening, from afel (dark, late). The wheat and spelt's survival means Egypt's food supply is not yet fully destroyed; the agricultural crisis is severe but not yet total. This restraint — even in the most destructive plague so far — is consistent with the graduated purpose (Exodus 9:15-16): YHWH is demonstrating power, not annihilating Egypt prematurely.
33: vayifros kapav el-YHWH: "He spread out his hands toward the LORD." The intercessory gesture (cf. Exodus 9:29) occurs outside the city — at a physical remove from Pharaoh's court, in the open field, facing the heavens. The spatial and social geography of the prayer matters: Moses does not pray in Pharaoh's presence but in YHWH's presence, turning from the human authority toward the divine.
34: vayosef lachato vayakhbeid libbo hu va'avadav: "he sinned again and hardened his heart, he and his officials." Kaved (H3515) — the final occurrence with Pharaoh as explicit subject. "He and his officials" — the hardening now encompasses the entire institutional court; it is not Pharaoh alone but the collective Egyptian ruling class that hardens together. The communal hardening prepares for communal judgment at the sea.
35: vayechezak lev Paroh velo shalach: "Chazaq — Pharaoh's heart was firm." The closing formula uses chazaq (the resolution-and-firmness verb) and ends with "just as the LORD had said through Moses" (kaasher diber YHWH beyad Mosheh). Every element of chapters 7-9 — prediction, execution, hardening — occurs "just as the LORD had said." The plague narrative is presented as the perfect fulfillment of YHWH's own announced plan.
